content.claim: The ministry administratively rejected a church-run school's request to launch an 8-grade gimnázium on segregation grounds, demonstrating that discretionary administrative tools can constrain selective-track expansion without new legislation, and that disadvantaged-family advocacy groups actively monitor and publicize such decisions.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Political feasibility lens
limitations: This is a single case and does not demonstrate systemic generalizability of administrative constraint as a reform method
The decision blocks *expansion* of an existing track type, not the existing 6- and 8-year gimnáziums themselves
Counter-coalition monitoring does not measure mobilization capacity or political salience sufficient to sustain legislative pressure
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
